items.title: Constitution Articles Quick Capsule: 14 Must-Recall Provisions
items.summary: A rapid-recall capsule of fourteen high-frequency constitutional articles, written as one-line pairings of article number and core fact for fast pre-exam revision.
items.body:
Run this capsule before any polity section. Read each line once, cover the fact on the right, and try to recall it from the article number alone. Aim to finish the full sweep in under three minutes, then repeat only the lines that hesitated.

Article 14 — guarantees equality before law and equal protection of laws to all persons within the territory of India.
Article 19 — grants six freedoms to citizens, including speech and expression, each subject to reasonable restrictions listed in the article itself.
Article 21 — protects life and personal liberty; courts have expanded it to cover privacy, dignity, and a clean environment.
Article 32 — the right to constitutional remedies, famously described by Ambedkar as the heart and soul of the Constitution.
Article 44 — directive principle urging the State to secure a uniform civil code for citizens across India.
Article 51A — lists the fundamental duties of citizens, inserted by the 42nd Amendment in 1976.
Article 72 — empowers the President to grant pardons, reprieves, respites, and remissions of punishment.
Article 110 — defines a money bill, which can be introduced only in the Lok Sabha on the President's recommendation.
Article 123 — allows the President to promulgate ordinances when Parliament is not in session.
Article 226 — empowers High Courts to issue writs for fundamental rights and for any other purpose, a reach wider than Article 32.
Article 280 — provides for a Finance Commission every five years to recommend tax devolution between the Union and the states.
Article 324 — vests the superintendence, direction, and control of elections in the Election Commission of India.
Article 356 — permits President's rule in a state upon failure of constitutional machinery.
Article 368 — lays down Parliament's power and the procedure to amend the Constitution.

If any line slows you down, flag it and attach a single keyword to the article number, such as equality for 14 or ordinance for 123. Match-the-following questions reward instant one-word recall far more than long descriptions, so drill the pairing rather than the paragraph.

items.title: Grammar Rules Quick Capsule: 14 Error-Spotting Essentials
items.summary: A one-line-per-rule capsule covering the grammar points most often tested in error spotting and sentence correction, built for a fast two-minute revision sweep.
items.body:
This capsule compresses the grammar rules that drive most error-spotting and sentence-correction questions. Read one line, pause, and frame a quick example of your own before moving to the next. Self-generated examples lock rules in far better than rereading.

Subject-verb agreement — a verb agrees with its true subject in number, not with words inside a phrase that sits between them.
Either-or pairing — when subjects are joined by either-or or neither-nor, the verb agrees with the subject nearer to it.
Collective nouns — take a singular verb when the group acts as one unit and a plural verb when members act separately.
Fewer versus less — use fewer for countable nouns and less for uncountable quantities such as time or money treated as bulk.
Since versus for — since marks a point of time while for marks a duration; both pair naturally with perfect tenses.
Each and every — pronouns and verbs that follow each, every, either, and neither stay singular in formal usage.
Comparative degree — use the comparative for two items and reserve the superlative for three or more.
Dangling modifier — an opening descriptive phrase must logically attach to the subject that immediately follows the comma.
Unreal conditional — improbable present conditions take past tense in the if-clause and would plus base verb in the main clause.
Reported speech — a past reporting verb shifts tenses, pronouns, and time expressions one step backward.
Article by sound — choose a or an by sound rather than spelling, so it is a university but an hour.
Fixed prepositions — different from, prefer to, comply with, and abide by are fixed pairings tested year after year.
Pronoun case — after prepositions and after the verb let, use object pronouns such as me, him, and them.
Parallelism — items in a list must share the same grammatical form, whether nouns, gerunds, or infinitives.

Close the sweep by rewriting any rule you fumbled as a wrong sentence and its correction. Seeing the error and the fix side by side mirrors exactly how the exam will test you.

items.title: Vocabulary Roots Quick Capsule: 14 Roots That Unlock Hundreds of Words
items.summary: A rapid capsule of fourteen Latin and Greek roots with sample words and meanings, letting you decode unfamiliar vocabulary in reading passages instead of memorising lists.
items.body:
Roots are multipliers: learn one and you can decode a dozen words you have never seen. Sweep this capsule once a week and test yourself by inventing a new example word for each root before reading the ones given.

bene — Latin for good or well; benevolent means kindly, a benefactor gives help, and a benediction is a blessing.
mal — bad or evil; to malign is to speak ill of someone, malevolent means wishing harm, and a malady is an illness.
chron — Greek for time; chronology orders events, a chronic problem lasts long, and an anachronism sits outside its proper time.
dict — to say or declare; dictate, verdict, contradict, and edict all involve formal speech or pronouncement.
spect — to look; a spectator watches, to inspect is to look closely into, and circumspect means cautious and watchful.
port — to carry; transport carries across, portable can be carried, and to export is to carry goods out of a country.
cred — to believe; credible can be believed, a credulous person believes too easily, and an incredulous one refuses to believe.
voc or vok — voice or call; an advocate speaks for someone, to invoke is to call upon, and vociferous means loudly insistent.
path — feeling or suffering; sympathy shares another's feeling, apathy is the absence of feeling, and pathos stirs pity.
graph or gram — writing; an autograph is self-written, a biography writes out a life, and a telegram carries a written message far.
phil — love; philanthropy is love of humankind, a bibliophile loves books, and philosophy is the love of wisdom.
anthrop — human; anthropology studies humans, a misanthrope dislikes people, and a philanthropist works for their good.
circum — around; a circumference runs around, to circumvent is to get around, and to circumscribe is to draw a limit around.
logy — study of; biology studies life, etymology studies word origins, and psychology studies the mind.

When a strange word appears in a passage, split it into root, prefix, and suffix before guessing. A reasoned guess from parts beats a blind one almost every time.

items.title: Economy Terms Quick Capsule: 14 Definitions Every Aspirant Must Hold
items.summary: One-line recall definitions of fourteen core economic terms, from repo rate to the Phillips curve, designed for a fast sweep before any economy or banking-awareness section.
items.body:
Economy questions usually test whether you can match a term to its one-line meaning under time pressure. This capsule gives you exactly that pairing. Cover the right side of each line and recall the definition from the term alone.

Repo rate — the rate at which the central bank lends short-term funds to banks against approved securities.
Reverse repo — the rate at which banks park surplus funds with the central bank; it sets a floor under money-market rates.
Cash reserve ratio — the slice of deposits banks must keep as cash with the central bank, earning no interest.
Statutory liquidity ratio — the share of deposits banks hold in liquid assets such as government securities and gold.
Fiscal deficit — total government spending minus total receipts excluding borrowings, usually quoted as a percentage of GDP.
Revenue deficit — the gap between revenue receipts and revenue spending; it signals borrowing for consumption rather than asset creation.
GDP deflator — nominal GDP divided by real GDP, the broadest single measure of price change in the economy.
CPI versus WPI — the consumer price index tracks retail prices households pay, while the wholesale index tracks bulk prices; monetary policy targets CPI.
Inflation targeting — a framework in which the central bank commits to keeping retail inflation inside a publicly announced band.
Current account — the record of trade in goods and services plus incomes and transfers with the rest of the world.
Disinvestment — the sale of government equity in public enterprises, either in small tranches or through strategic transfer of control.
Base effect — distortion in year-on-year inflation caused by unusually high or low prices in the same month a year earlier.
Liquidity trap — a state where interest rates are already so low that further monetary easing fails to lift spending.
Phillips curve — the classical short-run trade-off between inflation and unemployment.

After the sweep, pick any two related terms, such as repo and reverse repo, and explain aloud how they differ. Contrast-based recall is the fastest route to mastery in economy sections.

items.title: Geography Facts Quick Capsule: 14 Map-Ready Recall Lines
items.summary: A rapid-revision capsule of fourteen one-line geography facts on India's rivers, soils, lakes, channels, and monsoon, written for quick recall before static GK sections.
items.body:
Geography rewards picture-linked memory. As you read each line below, visualise the location on a mental map of India. A fact tied to a place on the map survives exam pressure far better than a fact tied to a page.

Tropic of Cancer — crosses eight Indian states, running from Gujarat in the west to Mizoram in the east.
Standard meridian — 82.5 degrees east, passing near Mirzapur, sets Indian Standard Time five and a half hours ahead of GMT.
Kangchenjunga — the third highest mountain in the world and the highest peak lying within India, on the Sikkim border.
Ganga — rises from the Gangotri glacier as the Bhagirathi and takes the name Ganga after meeting the Alaknanda at Devprayag.
Godavari — the largest peninsular river, often called the Dakshin Ganga, rising near Trimbak in Maharashtra.
Western Ghats — an almost continuous hill chain broken by the Palghat Gap, older and more eroded than the Himalayas.
Chilika — the largest brackish water lagoon in India, on the Odisha coast, a major winter home for migratory birds.
Wular — among the largest freshwater lakes in India, lying on the course of the Jhelum in Kashmir.
Ten Degree Channel — separates the Andaman group of islands from the Nicobar group.
Palk Strait — the narrow stretch of sea separating India from Sri Lanka near Rameswaram.
Monsoon onset — the southwest monsoon normally reaches the Kerala coast in the first week of June.
Black soil — also called regur, formed from basalt, strongly moisture-retentive, and ideal for cotton cultivation.
Laterite soil — develops under high heat and heavy rain where leaching removes silica, common along the Ghats and in the northeast.
Sundarbans — the vast mangrove delta built by the Ganga and the Brahmaputra, famous as the home of the Royal Bengal tiger.

Finish by sketching a rough outline of India from memory and placing five of these facts on it. The act of drawing, however crude, converts reading into durable spatial recall.

items.title: Important Committees and Bodies Capsule: 14 Institutions in One Line Each
items.summary: One-line recall facts on fourteen key constitutional, statutory, and executive bodies, with their legal basis and core function, for fast pre-exam revision.
items.body:
Questions on institutions almost always test one of three things: the legal basis of a body, its core function, or its head. Each line below packs the first two; add the current head from your own running list as you revise.

Election Commission — constitutional body under Article 324; superintends elections to Parliament, state legislatures, and the offices of President and Vice President.
Union Public Service Commission — constitutional recruiter under Articles 315 to 323 for all-India and central services.
Comptroller and Auditor General — constitutional auditor under Article 148, often called the guardian of the public purse.
Finance Commission — constituted every five years under Article 280 to recommend the sharing of taxes between the Union and the states.
GST Council — constitutional body under Article 279A, chaired by the Union finance minister, deciding rates and rules for the goods and services tax.
Attorney General — the highest law officer of the country under Article 76, with the right of audience in all courts of India.
NITI Aayog — an executive policy think tank that replaced the Planning Commission; it is neither constitutional nor statutory.
Reserve Bank of India — the statutory central bank created by a 1934 law; it issues currency and conducts monetary policy.
SEBI — the statutory regulator of the securities market, protecting investors and supervising exchanges and intermediaries.
NHRC — the statutory human rights watchdog under the Protection of Human Rights Act, headed by a former Supreme Court judge.
Central Vigilance Commission — the statutory anti-corruption body, first set up on the recommendation of the Santhanam Committee.
CBI — the investigative agency drawing its powers from the Delhi Special Police Establishment Act; it needs state consent for many cases.
Law Commission — a non-statutory advisory panel that recommends reform and codification of laws.
NABARD — the statutory apex institution for agricultural and rural credit, supervising regional rural and cooperative banks.

While revising, sort these bodies into three mental buckets: constitutional, statutory, and executive. Classification questions on this exact distinction appear in nearly every static GK section.

items.title: Current Affairs Themes Checklist Capsule: 14 Buckets to Sweep Every Week
items.summary: A weekly checklist capsule of fourteen recurring current-affairs themes, each with a one-line cue on exactly what to recall, so no high-yield bucket goes unrevised.
items.body:
Current affairs revision fails when it is unstructured. Instead of rereading everything, sweep these fourteen fixed buckets once a week and ask the recall question written against each one. The buckets stay the same; only the content inside them changes.

Monetary policy review — recall the policy rate direction, the stated stance, and the inflation projection from the latest statement.
Budget and taxation — revise the major allocation heads, the fiscal deficit target, and any new schemes or slabs announced.
Summits and groupings — for each grouping such as G20, BRICS, or SCO, recall the members, the current chair, and the host nation.
Reports and indices — for each global index, fix the publishing body in memory; examiners test the publisher more than the numbers.
Awards and honours — cluster winners by field, such as literature, science, sport, and civilian honours, rather than by date.
Defence exercises — link every joint exercise name to the partner country and the service branch involved.
Space and science — track the launch vehicle, the mission objective, and the agency responsible rather than exact dates.
Sports events — recall the trophy, the sport, the winning side, and the venue for marquee tournaments.
Appointments — connect each person to the institution and, where possible, to the predecessor.
Schemes and policy — for each new scheme, fix the ministry, the target group, and the funding pattern.
Environment and climate — revise convention names, recurring conference themes, and the country's stated commitments.
Books and authors — pair the title with the author and one phrase about the subject.
Persons in news — link each name to a single field of contribution; one anchor fact is enough.
Days and themes — international days sit on fixed dates; revise the observing body and the purpose of each.

At the end of the sweep, mark any bucket where you recalled nothing recent. That empty bucket, not the full ones, tells you exactly where next week's reading should begin.
